REGEX "meta-characters"

^     start the pattern at the beginning of the text
.     matches any single character (the same as ? in the wildcard "language" in Bash)
$     end the pattern at the end of the text


NOTE:   ^ and $ together are known as "anchors". Because the "anchor" the pattern to the
        beginning (ie for ^) or the end (i.e. for $) of the text being searched.



Pattern:    ^p.y$
Meaning:    any text that starts with p
            followed by a single character
            folled by a y (which is the end of the text being searched)


*     a * that comes after part of a pattern means to repeat that part of the pattern zero or more times

EXMAPLES

Pattern:   ^p.*y$       # in this example the * modifies the . (i.e. zero or more . meta characters)
Meaning:   start the text with a p (^p)
           any character - zero or more times (.*)
           end the text with a y (y$)

Pattern:   ^pa*y$        #  in this example, the * modifies the "a"  (i.e. zero or more a's)
Matches:   py     # zero a's
           pay    # one a
           paay   # two a's
           paaay  # etc ...


NOTE - the following "character classes" (i.e. [bracketed characters]) is the same notation for BOTH
Bash's wildcard language for matching filenames as well as for REGEX patterns for matching data

[abc]      matches exactly one a,b or c
[a-f]      same as [abcdef]
[A-F]      same as [ABCDEF]
[0-9]      same as [0123456789]
[a-fq0-3]  same as [abcdefq0123]
[^abc]     any single character that is NOT an a,b or c (that include any single letter, digit or symbol, e.g. d,e,0,1,?,!,space, etc)
    `
